mnemonics.title: PAGE REPLACEMENT ALGORITHMS
mnemonics.content: FIFO: FIRST IN FIRST OUT - OLDEST PAGE LEAVES | LRU: LEAST RECENTLY USED - OLDEST IN TIME GETS ACCUSED | OPTIMAL: OBSERVES FUTURE, PICKS PERFECTLY | CLOCK: CIRCULAR BUFFER HANDS SECOND CHANCES

mnemonics.title: DISK SCHEDULING ALGORITHMS ORDER
mnemonics.content: F S S C - FCFS THEN SSTF THEN SCAN THEN C-SCAN | FAST CARS ZIGZAG: FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK

mnemonics.title: VIRTUAL MEMORY BENEFITS
mnemonics.content: LARGER THAN RAM - ALL PROGRAMS CAN RUN | PROTECTION - EACH PROCESS ISOLATED | EFFICIENT - ONLY NEEDED PAGES LOADED | SHARING - COMMON CODE SHARED

mnemonics.title: TLB PURPOSE
mnemonics.content: TRANSLATION LOOKASIDE BUFFER: FAST ADDRESS TRANSLATION CACHE | REDUCES MEMORY ACCESSES FOR PAGE TABLE LOOKUPS

acronyms.usage: Hardware cache storing recent page table entries for fast virtual-to-physical address translation
Hardware component that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses
Page replacement algorithm that evicts the oldest page in memory
Page replacement algorithm that evicts least recently accessed page
Disk scheduling algorithm that services request closest to current head position
Disk scheduling that moves head in one direction then reverses
Disk scheduling that returns to beginning after servicing one direction
Page replacement selecting page not used for longest time

visualTips.visualization: Think of a book divided into fixed-size chapters (pages). Each chapter can be read from any physical location (frame) in the library (RAM). The table of contents (page table) tells you where each chapter is stored.
Think of a book with different sections (chapters, appendices, index) - each of variable size. Each section has a starting page number (base) and length (limit) that the library tracks separately.
Imagine a library with limited reading desks (RAM). When all desks are full, librarians (OS) temporarily move some books to storage (swap space) to free up desks, bringing them back when needed.
Imagine a student constantly swapping books in and out of a bag because they can't fit all needed books at once - spending more time swapping than actually studying (reading).
Think of a dictionary with frequently looked-up words marked with sticky notes (TLB entries) for quick reference instead of searching through the entire dictionary each time.
SCAN is like an elevator in a building going up servicing all floor requests, reaching the top, then going down to service remaining requests.

analogies.analogy: A map or index in a large library that tells you which shelf (frame) contains each book chapter (page) you need.
A virtual desktop where you have more application windows open than can fit on your physical screen - windows that are minimized are temporarily stored off-screen.
Trying to fit a large group at a restaurant but the tables are scattered - there's enough total space but not in one contiguous area.
Ordering a pizza in fixed sizes when you need a specific amount - you might get more than needed (wasted space).
A delivery person constantly driving back and forth to fetch one package at a time instead of collecting multiple packages in one trip - inefficient and time-consuming.
The specific textbooks and notes you need on your desk for today's study session - keeping only what's actively being used within reach.
